Scenario.title: Spear-Phishing Driven Cyber Attack on Energy Sector Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Energy Complex A sophisticated multi-stage attack targeting energy sector infrastructure leverages OSINT, phishing, and zero-day exploitation to compromise systems and disrupt operations.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Public Information Source, Process, Internet-facing assets, Target System Artifact, Windows Registry, User Account, Network Traffic, File, Domain Name Registrar, OS Process
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT) Gathering, Reg.exe Utility, General Access Method, Exploit TEE Vulnerability, Windows Registry Key Creation (Mechanism), User Account Authentication (Mechanism), Network Traffic Content (Mechanism), Process Creation (Mechanism), Pass the Hash, Data Manipulation

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attackers gather publicly available information about the energy company's infrastructure, including employee details and internet-facing assets, to identify potential vulnerabilities and targets.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC-635: Alternative Execution Due to Deceptive Filenames
stage.description: The threat actors create a malicious file disguised as a legitimate process using the Reg.exe utility, which is then hosted on a free platform to serve as a phishing landing page.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: The attackers deliver a spear-phishing campaign tailored to employees, exploiting human error to gain initial access through compromised web services and protocol manipulation.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC-67: String Format Overflow in syslog()
stage.description: The attackers exploit an unpatched Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) vulnerability in the target system to achieve remote code execution, establishing a foothold in the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: A scheduled task is created in the Windows Registry to maintain persistent access, ensuring the attackers retain control even after rebooting the compromised system.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: The attackers perform password spraying attacks across multiple user accounts to guess valid credentials and escalate privileges within the network.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC-645: Use of Captured Tickets (Pass The Ticket)
stage.description: The threat actors use stolen Kerberos tickets to bypass endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions, evading network monitoring tools undetected.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-670: Software Development Tools Maliciously Altered
stage.description: Maliciously modified deployment tools are executed to extract credentials stored in memory, granting the attackers access to sensitive account information.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: The attackers use network sniffing techniques with the Reg.exe utility to map internal network structures and identify critical systems for further exploitation.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: Stolen credentials are used in pass-the-hash attacks to move laterally across the domain, accessing additional systems and resources.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: The attackers modify files on compromised systems to exfiltrate sensitive operational data, including proprietary energy grid configurations.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Data is exfiltrated using IDE tunneling techniques, masking malicious traffic within legitimate process creation activities to avoid detection.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: The final stage involves manipulating industrial control system data to disrupt energy grid operations, causing operational downtime and potential safety risks.

Scenario.summary: This complex attack chain demonstrates how basic-skilled adversaries can exploit OSINT, phishing, and zero-day vulnerabilities to compromise energy sector infrastructure, ultimately leading to operational disruption through targeted data manipulation.
